title: Copperplate, shade and hairline fundamentals
subtitle: The pressure-and-release mechanics of the pointed nib
excerpt: Copperplate uses a flexible pointed nib that produces thick strokes through pressure and thin strokes by releasing that pressure. The thick-and-thin logic is entirely different from the broad-edge nib: there is no nib-angle geometry, only pressure control. This tutorial establishes the five basic strokes that underlie the entire Copperplate alphabet.
sourceNotes: Edward Cocker, The Pen's Triumph (1657), and related seventeenth-century English roundhand copybooks, public domain by age. The Copperplate tradition derives from English roundhand, which is documented in Cocker's manuals and in the Spencer Brothers' penmanship materials of the nineteenth century. Platt Rogers Spencer, The Spencerian Key to Practical Penmanship (1866), is public domain. Internet Archive: archive.org/details/spenceriankeytop00spen.

glossaryTerms.definition: The thick downstroke of a Copperplate letter, produced by applying firm downward pressure on the flexible pointed nib so that the two tines spread and lay a wide ink band.
The thin stroke of a Copperplate letter, produced with minimal pressure, the two nib tines close together, creating the thinnest line the nib can produce.
The forward lean of Copperplate letterforms, typically 54° from the baseline, much more extreme than Italic's 5 to 10°.

content.text: Copperplate is a pressure-responsive hand. The pointed flexible nib has two tines that spread apart under downward pressure to produce a wide
and close together when pressure is released to produce a
of 54° from the baseline requires the oblique pen holder, which angles the nib to the right so a right-handed writer can pull the nib at the correct angle without contorting the wrist.

attrs.title: Surface preparation
attrs.body: Pointed-nib Copperplate is sensitive to surface texture and oils. Dust the practice sheet lightly with gum sandarac powder, tap off the excess, and wipe with a clean cloth. This prevents ink from spreading into the paper fibres and helps hairlines stay crisp.

items.name: Nikko G nib (stiff, controlled, the best starting nib for Copperplate)
Iron-gall ink, or, calligraphy gouache thinned to ink consistency
Cartridge paper, 130 gsm, with guide lines at 54° slope and 3.5 mm x-height
Gum sandarac (optional)
items.qty: Small amount in a muslin sachet for dusting the paper surface

content.text: Full shade (the oval shade). The fundamental thick stroke. Hold the pen at the Copperplate slope angle. Place the nib on the paper and apply steady downward pressure as the stroke descends. The tines spread. At the foot of the stroke, release pressure as the nib lifts. The shade should be even in width from top to bottom, with clean edges and no ragged spray.

Hairline upstroke. Hold the nib with minimal pressure. Lift the nib slightly, the tines barely touch the paper, and draw upward and to the right at the Copperplate slope. The stroke should be the thinnest line possible. If the hairline has any visible width, reduce the pressure further.

Oval. Begin at the 1-o'clock position with minimal pressure. Curve up and over, applying gentle increasing pressure on the left downstroke, then releasing completely on the upstroke to the right. The oval produces a hairline at the top and right, and a shade at the left and base.

Underturn. Begin with a shade on the downstroke; at the foot of the stroke, release pressure entirely and curve upward to the right in a hairline. The transition from shade to hairline at the baseline is the most important moment in Copperplate. Do not begin releasing too early; do not leave it too late.

Overturn. Begin with a hairline upstroke from the baseline; at the x-height line, apply pressure and begin the downstroke. The shade begins at the peak of the curve. This is the reverse of the underturn.

items.symptom: The nib catches and spatters on the upstroke hairlines
items.cause: The nib is being pushed (moved upward against the nib tip) rather than pulled. Pushing a pointed nib catches the tine corners on paper fibres.
items.fix: Copperplate hairline upstrokes must be drawn at an angle, not straight up, but upward and to the right, following the Copperplate slope. This oblique angle keeps the nib on the heel of the tines rather than the corners. If the nib still catches, try rotating the paper slightly so the upstroke direction feels more natural.

items.symptom: The shade is uneven, thicker in the middle than at the ends
items.cause: Pressure is being applied after the stroke has begun and released before it ends, instead of building from the first contact.
items.fix: Bring the nib to the paper already at the shade pressure. The shade starts at the start of the stroke, not halfway through. Practise the pressure change off the paper on a flat surface first: press the tines open and draw the motion before committing ink.
